feed.title: blank
feed.subtitle: Urban Sensing & Analytics Lab · University of Glasgow

entry.title: 英国教职五年谈
entry.content:
本文首发于知乎。English version: read here.

一转眼已经 2024 年年底了,在英国和美国生活的时间会在25年1月持平,真是时光荏苒啊。

回头看看,今年从工作到生活上都是一个极其忙碌的年份。今年非常幸运在格大晋升为了城市分析的正教授,也想稍微总结一下过去五年。一方面是给自己留个记录,另一方面也给有兴趣来英国或者欧洲学术界工作的朋友一些参考。美帝那边川宝再来四年,加上孤立主义整体复燃和中美关系摩擦不断,估计现在大家都会全世界看看机会。

五年半以前从美国来英国的时候其实没想那么多,但是到欧洲以后发现其实这确实是一个相当不同的大陆。虽然都讲英语,英国和美国祖上有点血缘关系,美国公司也喜欢到英国开分舵和买买买,但是整体社会组织形式和思想观念还是有很大不同。在中美待久了就会有一种天然的大国习惯感,市场大体量大,啥啥领域都要做都要竞争。但是到了欧洲就发现每个国家就那么点大,英国脱欧,欧盟还是一个不那么团结的实体。和朋友聊天的时候经常说如果把欧洲当成一个整体和中美对比,可能还能在饮食,旅游,天气,以及其他方面稍微竞争对比一下。但是当我今年在曼城开会的时候,在一家亚美尼亚餐厅甩出这个问题给一桌欧陆同事,他们的表情全是。。后面的对话就是我们欧洲国家怎么可能是一个整体,这几辈子都不可能发生。

算了,反正欧洲分裂一点也好,这样能保持欧洲大陆全世界最好的文化多样性和特色,也不是什么坏事。

回到英国来说,这国家大概快七千万人,绝大多数在英格兰(五千多万),苏格兰大概有英格兰一半大,但是只有五百多万人,也就是十分之一的人口。整个英国的陆地面积大概和 Michigan 差不多大,而美国还有 10 个州比 Michigan 大。更别说那个小红书上英国就是中国陕西省的笑话了。产业方面英国是以金融为主,IT 方面有美国大厂的分部 (比如 Google Deepmind) 和一些小厂,还有生物制药,能源,化工,和一些高端制造业还在运行。所以在英国读完书想找工作确实没那么容易,一方面是国家就这么大,产业和工作机会就这么多,是不可能和美国或者中国一样可以全美国全中国找工作跳槽搬家的。欧洲人因为可以在欧盟内部随便找工作,加上语言有一定的优势,相对来说会好很多,但是对于国际生来说这确实和中美两个大国比起来是个劣势。

欧洲的高校教职,因为英语的原因,英国和爱尔兰有天生的优势来接纳外来移民老师。欧陆国家里荷兰应该是非常好的选择,英语普及率极高,但是这几年慢慢也开始保守起来,要求越来越多荷兰语教学。其他的主要国家比如德法瑞士奥地利比利时和北欧三国,大学质量很高,但是一般都要求 3-5年以后要本地语言教学。如果大家现在还年轻,多学点语言没错的,实在不行推推下一代也是个办法。。办公室隔壁同事可以英德西葡四种语言流利通吃,走遍大半个欧洲都谈笑风生。除了语言优势以外,英国的教职系统相对来说对年轻老师更加友好,毕竟很多欧陆国家还在一个系一个教授的系统里打转。

英国教职可以和美国或者香港新加坡Tenure-track AP对标的类型叫做 Research & Teaching Track Lecturer,就是统称研究教学岗讲师。加拿大教职系统基本上已经按照美国的系统改革了,澳洲和新西兰很多学校还是遵循了英联邦的习惯。这边也经常会招聘一些纯教学的岗位,叫做 Learning & Teaching Track,title 完全一样,但是主要以教学为主。大多数R&T track 的职位和一部分 LTS track 的职位会以Permanent position的形式来招,可以大概认为是入职 Tenured,在大学没有财务危机的情况下工作职位是永久而且相对稳定的。如果有机会拿到英国 Russell Group或者是行业内顶尖高校的 R&T Lecturer,完全是值得考虑来的。英国还有 Research-only 的岗位,从助理研究员到博士后研究员,再到高级研究员,有的学校还有教授级研究员。虽然 Research-only 的岗位的合同不是永久的,但是和另外两个 track 的岗位一样是可以晋升的。我个人不太清楚美国是否有资深研究员可以直接拿到 tenured 教授岗位,但是英国确实是有资深研究员直接转成教授的情况。相对来说英国的教职体系的包容性更强,某些方面更人性化一些。

英国教职比较好的地方就是没有 Tenure-track 的压力,所以相对来说考核的压力没有那么大,可以从入职开始就做一些自己感兴趣的研究。新老师一般会有一个半年到最多四年的试用期(取决于不同的学校),但是几乎没有听说有谁会通过不了。考核的时候是格大是基于本专业学科内部考核,横向对比另外 23 所罗素集团大学的成果。考核的规则主要有七项,包括论文,科研经费。博士生培养,教学,领导力,社会影响力,和在领域内的声望和知名度(Esteem,不知道要怎么翻译)。论文非常强调质量,既不看期刊分区也不看影响因子,全看学科评估的 Peer review(全是谜)。科研经费这年头在哪里竞争都激烈,但是可以申请的口子不少,包括英国基金委UKRI,欧盟地平线Horizon Europe,欧空局ESA,英国以及全球的 Charities 比如 Wellcome,Levelhulme,Royal Society,甚至公司和政府的横向等等。教学任务整体来说不算多,新老师一般是 2 门课左右。英国的评价体系非常强调研究的社会影响力impact,从某种意义上拉近了学术研究和社会的距离。

当然英国教职也有一些劣势,第一就是年轻老师工资比起美国R1 R2 香港新加坡低不少,比起加拿大澳洲新西兰也没啥优势。因为欧洲在 08 年金融危机以后基本上经济增长停滞,导致每年英国全国大学的集体工资涨薪谈判最终都不能够达到通胀水平(典型的欧洲大锅饭主义),就算一年少 1-2%,十几年积累下来大概也会 underpay 20-30%,更不用说欧洲普遍的高税收福利社会制度和英镑贬值了。在英国高校工资相对较高的主要是在伦敦的帝国理工,伦敦政经,还有伦敦商学院等等。但是在欧洲范围来说,其实大家都半斤八两,除了瑞士是个高薪低税的地方。第二是新老师没有什么 startup,基本上给点小钱就打发了,入职以后的起步会慢一些。逻辑其实也好理解,因为招聘进来就是永久教职,而不像 tenure track 系统希望能够从新老师身上得到明确的收益,自然直接给的 startup 投资会相对少一些。第三个是博士生方面。因为英国的博士学制短,奖学金一般只有 3-4 年(英国政府 3-3.5 年,csc 4 年),这样就导致博士生的容错率比较低,如果头一两年做得不顺利,后面要延期个半年到一年是可以的,但是奖学金发完就没了,财务方面会比较麻烦。而且因为博士年限短,一般博士毕业都得做博后或者申请博后 fellowship 再积累成果,比较难博士毕业直接拿到教职。由于英国政府的博士奖学金只有最多 30%的名额可以给国际生,但是英国本地人对于读博其实并不是特别感冒,就会导致国际生奖学金竞争十分激烈,但是只能招本地生的70%博士生奖学金招不到人。我们中心城市大数据方向过去 5 年印象里就没有招过英国籍的博士生。第四是研究经费方面。由于英国非常强调跨学科合作,基本上大多数中等到大型的项目都很强调跨学科合作,需要新老师能积极主动的建立人际关系和网络,相对来说会比较难找到像美国 NSF 那样专心支持基础研究的经费机会。最后就是最近英国大学普遍因为政府限制硕士学生配偶签导致国际学生减少而陷入财政危机,纷纷开始节衣缩食和裁员。但是因为工会的努力,绝大多数大学都是以自愿离职给补偿的方式裁员或者停止招聘。

虽然日不落帝国早都日落了,但是留下来的文化影响力还是处处可见。经常可以看到英国的老师跳槽到别的英联邦国家,港新,以及美国 (美国教职一般需要美国 phd 或者欧洲顶尖学校 phd,不然需要做博后或者有以前的美国经历,当然大牛想去哪就能去哪,这就不杠了)。由于脱欧的原因,欧陆的人口和英国不再能自由流动,但是 Global Talent Visa 对于科研人员特别是博后很友好,三年就可以拿永居,英国永居也没有美国绿卡那么多税收和居住条件的限制。英国房子相对于收入来说不便宜,但是一般来说能够享受比较好的城市生活。

再谈谈自己过去五年的一些总结和未来的展望,我觉得可能有这几点值得提一下:

我们的城市分析硕士项目从无到有,每年都有奖学金来支持苏格兰本地学生的培养,以及已经运行了两年的和芬兰Aalto 大学的交换项目。后面我们会继续拓展和欧洲大陆的合作交流,希望能有更多的硕士和博士的联合培养项目。MSc Urban Analytics

过去五年做了不少和基础城市数据相关的研究,包括数据的生产,处理,分发,和拓展。深切的体会到未来数据肯定还是核心资产,LLM 的算法代码权重都可以开源,但是训练数据开源的真不多。Data Paper 1 Data Paper 2

在中心做起来了一个城市传感的方向 UBDC Urban Sensing,开展了不少七七八八的和 Sensing 相关的项目。未来应该会继续结合城市传感来推动城市可持续发展和低碳零碳方向,主要集中在交通 Air quality has improved in Low Emission Zones 和住房领域 Building housing energy efficiency models from local sensing。

这些项目里最好玩的应该是我们Sensor-enhanced housing survey项目,买传感器加上 3D 打印外壳都挺好玩。Sensor-enhanced housing surveys 还有跨学科的城市语言学的项目也很有趣。New geographies of language in multilingual cities

疫情期间做了不少短期的 rapid response项目,最主要是关于手机 app 数据的分析和实践,积累了不少项目的经验。Methodological challenges of digital footprint data for mobility analysis

和朋友同事一起张罗了三年 Google Summer of Code,攒出来一个不错的空间优化的 python 开源包(主要是 Facility Location Modeling 部分)。未来还要继续努力更新。PySAL-spopt

和英国媒体打交道:23 年的传感器项目被英国镜报 UK facing summer health crisis as half of UK homes hit ‘sauna-like’ temperatures,还有很多别的媒体报道,最终的研究报告也被伦敦 Southwark Council 作为政策制定的参考 Council Report。24 年格拉斯哥 Low Emission Zone 的文章被 BBC Scotland Radio采访,还有不少本地的媒体报道 Glasgow LEZ。感受到媒体的力量同时,又对媒体所传达信息更加谨慎。

帮忙组织了大大小小的几个国际和本地会议,包括 GIScience 2023, CPGIS 2023, GISRUK 2023, AGILE 2024, Turing Urban Analytics Event 2024, Data-driven Decarbonisation in Scotland等等。欢迎大家来格拉斯哥玩,虽然我们的天气真不咋地。。

因为给盖茨基金会做的尼日利亚和肯尼亚的城市贫民窟项目,进一步开拓了非洲市场。The IDEAMAPS Network - Integrated Deprived Area Mapping System

第一个 PhD Niantang 顺利毕业,未来发展一切顺利!

从我个人来说,五年的时间不长也不短,更何况还经历了三年疫情。我觉得做研究的最重要的品质还是内在的Interest和Resilience/Discipline。毕竟论文尤其是项目被拒早都是常态了,没有点兴趣和韧性很难坚持下来。在此之外如果还能更 smart 一点那是最好了。时间管理非常重要,要少看短视频。未来 3-5 年希望继续做更有趣的研究,也能进一步推动我们的本科硕士博士生培养。感谢这些年所有帮助过我的人,还有和我一起工作的博士后,博士生和硕士生们。一个人的力量总是有限的,一群人的力量就不一样了。
entry.summary: 首发于知乎的中文原文;English version available.

entry.title: Reflections on Five Years as UK Faculty

link.title: Reflections on Five Years as UK Faculty

entry.content:
Translated from my original Chinese post on Zhihu (published December 2024). 中文版

In the blink of an eye it is the end of 2024 — by January 2025, the time I have lived in the UK will equal the time I lived in the US. Time really does fly.

Looking back, this has been an extremely busy year for both work and life. I was very fortunate to be promoted to full Professor of Urban Analytics at Glasgow this year, so I want to take stock of the past five years — partly to leave a record for myself, and partly as a reference for friends interested in working in UK or European academia. With Trump back for another four years in the US, isolationism resurgent, and constant friction in China–US relations, I suspect many people are now looking at opportunities all over the world.

When I moved from the US to the UK five and a half years ago I did not think about it too deeply, but after arriving I discovered that Europe really is a rather different continent. Both countries speak English, Britain and America share some ancestry, and American companies love opening branches in — and buying up — the UK; yet the overall way society is organised, and the mindset, are quite different. If you have spent a long time in China or the US, you develop an instinctive “big country” mentality: huge markets, huge scale, competing in every field imaginable. In Europe, you realise each country is only so big; Britain has left the EU, and the EU itself is not an especially united entity. I used to joke with friends that if you treated Europe as a single whole, it might just about compete with China and the US on food, travel, weather, and a few other fronts. But when I threw that question at a table of continental colleagues in an Armenian restaurant during a conference in Manchester this year, their faces said it all. The conversation that followed was: “How could we European countries possibly be one whole? Not in several lifetimes.”

Fair enough — perhaps a somewhat fragmented Europe is no bad thing, since it preserves the best cultural diversity and distinctiveness in the world.

Back to the UK: this is a country of nearly seventy million people, the great majority in England (fifty-odd million). Scotland is about half the size of England but has only a bit over five million people — a tenth of the population. The whole UK is roughly the size of Michigan, and ten US states are bigger than Michigan — never mind the joke on Xiaohongshu that Britain is the size of Shaanxi province. Industrially, the UK is dominated by finance; in IT there are branches of the big American firms (Google DeepMind, for example) and some smaller companies, plus biopharma, energy, chemicals, and some high-end manufacturing still running. So finding a job in the UK after graduating really is not that easy: the country is only so big, and there are only so many industries and openings — you cannot job-hop and relocate across an entire continent the way you can in the US or China. Europeans can work anywhere in the EU and have a language advantage, so they fare much better; for international students, this genuinely is a disadvantage compared with the two big countries.

For academic jobs in Europe, English gives the UK and Ireland a natural advantage in absorbing immigrant academics. On the continent, the Netherlands should be an excellent choice — English is almost universal — though in recent years it has been growing more conservative, with increasing demands for teaching in Dutch. Other major countries such as Germany, France, Switzerland, Austria, Belgium, and the Nordics have very good universities, but usually require you to teach in the local language after 3–5 years. If you are still young, learning more languages never hurts — or failing that, push the next generation to. A colleague in the office next door is fluent in English, German, Spanish, and Portuguese, and glides through half of Europe with ease. Beyond the language advantage, the UK academic system is also relatively friendly to early-career staff — many continental systems still revolve around one professor per department.

The UK position comparable to a tenure-track assistant professorship in the US, Hong Kong, or Singapore is the Research & Teaching (R&T) Track Lecturer. Canada has largely reformed its system along American lines, while many universities in Australia and New Zealand still follow Commonwealth conventions. UK universities also frequently hire teaching-focused positions on the Learning & Teaching Track — identical titles, but primarily teaching. Most R&T posts, and some L&T posts, are advertised as permanent positions — you can roughly think of it as being tenured on arrival: as long as the university is not in financial crisis, the job is permanent and relatively stable. If you can land an R&T Lectureship at a UK Russell Group university or a top school in your field, it is absolutely worth considering. The UK also has research-only positions, from research assistant and postdoctoral researcher up to senior researcher — and at some universities, professorial research fellows. Research-only contracts are not permanent, but they are promotable just like the other two tracks. I am not sure whether senior researchers in the US can convert directly to tenured professorships, but in the UK there certainly are cases of senior researchers becoming professors. Overall, the UK system is more inclusive, and in some respects more humane.

The best part of a UK academic job is the absence of tenure-track pressure. The assessment pressure is comparatively low, and you can start doing research you actually find interesting from day one. New staff usually have a probation period of six months to at most four years (depending on the university), but I have almost never heard of anyone failing it. At Glasgow, assessment is done within your own discipline, benchmarked against the other 23 Russell Group universities. There are seven main criteria: publications, research funding, PhD supervision, teaching, leadership, societal impact, and esteem within the field. For publications the emphasis is squarely on quality — neither journal tiers nor impact factors count; it all comes down to peer review in the national research assessment (a complete enigma). Research funding is fiercely competitive everywhere these days, but there are plenty of doors to knock on: UKRI, the EU’s Horizon Europe, the European Space Agency, UK and global charities such as Wellcome, Leverhulme, and the Royal Society, and even industry and government contracts. The teaching load is generally not heavy — around two courses for new staff. And the UK evaluation system’s strong emphasis on societal impact has, in a sense, brought academic research closer to society.

Of course, UK academia has its downsides. First, early-career salaries are considerably lower than at US R1/R2 universities or in Hong Kong and Singapore, and hold no real advantage over Canada, Australia, or New Zealand. Since the 2008 financial crisis Europe’s growth has essentially stagnated, and the annual UK-wide university pay negotiations consistently fail to keep up with inflation (classic European egalitarianism); losing 1–2% a year compounds to roughly 20–30% underpayment over a decade or more — to say nothing of Europe’s generally high-tax welfare systems and the depreciation of the pound. The relatively better-paying UK institutions are mostly in London — Imperial, LSE, London Business School — but across Europe everyone is much of a muchness, with Switzerland the one high-salary, low-tax exception. Second, new staff get essentially no start-up package — a token amount at best — so the start is slower. The logic is understandable: you are hired into a permanent job, so unlike a tenure-track system that wants a clear return on a new hire, the up-front investment is smaller. Third, the PhD system: British PhDs are short, with funding typically only 3–4 years (UK government 3–3.5 years; CSC 4 years), which leaves little room for error — if the first year or two go badly you can extend by six months to a year, but once the funding runs out, finances get difficult. And because the PhD is short, graduates generally need a postdoc or a postdoctoral fellowship to accumulate a track record — it is hard to land a faculty job straight out of a PhD. At most 30% of UK government PhD studentships can go to international students, yet domestic students are not particularly keen on doing PhDs — so competition for the international quota is ferocious while the domestic 70% goes unfilled. In our centre’s urban big data area, I cannot recall recruiting a single British PhD student in the past five years. Fourth, research funding: the UK strongly emphasises interdisciplinarity — most medium-to-large grants demand cross-disciplinary collaboration and proactive network-building — and it is comparatively hard to find funding purely dedicated to basic research like the US NSF’s. Finally, UK universities have lately fallen into financial crisis as government restrictions on master’s students’ dependant visas cut international enrolment, prompting belt-tightening and job cuts everywhere — though thanks to the unions, most universities have relied on voluntary severance schemes or hiring freezes.

The empire’s sun set long ago, but its cultural influence is everywhere: UK academics routinely move to other Commonwealth countries, Hong Kong and Singapore, and the US (US faculty jobs generally require a US PhD or one from a top European school, otherwise a postdoc or prior US experience — though stars can go wherever they like). Because of Brexit, people can no longer move freely between the continent and Britain, but the Global Talent Visa is very friendly to researchers, especially postdocs — permanent residency after three years, and UK permanent residency carries none of the tax and residence constraints of a US green card. Housing is not cheap relative to income, but in general you can enjoy a rather good urban life.

Now for a few reflections on my own past five years, and the road ahead:

  • Our MSc in Urban Analytics grew from nothing, with scholarships every year to support Scottish students, and an exchange programme with Aalto University in Finland now in its second year. We will keep expanding links with continental Europe, hopefully towards more joint master’s and doctoral training.
  • I did a good deal of research on fundamental urban data — its production, processing, distribution, and extension. It has really sunk in that data will remain the core asset: LLM code and weights can be open-sourced, but genuinely open training data is rare.
  • I built up an urban sensing direction at the centre (UBDC Urban Sensing) with a miscellany of sensing projects. Going forward this will connect to urban sustainability and low-carbon/net-zero work, mainly in transport (air quality in Low Emission Zones) and housing (building energy efficiency models from local sensing).
  • The most fun of these was our sensor-enhanced housing survey — buying sensors and 3D-printing enclosures was a delight. The interdisciplinary multilingual cities project on new geographies of language was fascinating too.
  • During the pandemic I ran several short rapid-response projects, mostly analysing mobile phone app data, which taught me a great deal about delivering projects.
  • With friends and colleagues I organised Google Summer of Code for three years, building up a solid open-source Python package for spatial optimisation (PySAL/spopt), mainly the facility location modelling part. More updates to come.
  • I learnt to work with the UK media: our 2023 sensor project was covered by the Mirror (“UK facing summer health crisis as half of UK homes hit ‘sauna-like’ temperatures”) and many other outlets, and the final research report informed policy at London’s Southwark Council. In 2024 our Glasgow Low Emission Zone paper led to a BBC Radio Scotland interview and plenty of local coverage. I felt the power of the media — and became more careful about how information gets conveyed.
  • I helped organise conferences big and small: GIScience 2023, CPGIS 2023, GISRUK 2023, AGILE 2024, the Turing Urban Analytics Event 2024, Data-driven Decarbonisation in Scotland, and more. Do come visit Glasgow — even if our weather really is nothing to write home about.
  • The Gates Foundation-funded work on informal settlements in Nigeria and Kenya opened up our engagement with Africa (the IDEAMAPS Network).
  • My first PhD student, Niantang, graduated smoothly — all the best for the future!

For me personally, five years is neither long nor short — and three of them were pandemic years. I have come to believe the most important qualities for research are intrinsic interest and resilience/discipline: rejections of papers, and especially of grant proposals, are simply routine, and without genuine interest and toughness it is hard to keep going. Being a bit smarter on top of that is a bonus. Time management matters enormously — watch fewer short videos. In the next 3–5 years I hope to keep doing more interesting research and to further strengthen our undergraduate, master’s, and doctoral training. My thanks to everyone who has helped me over these years, and to the postdocs, PhD students, and master’s students who work alongside me. One person’s strength is always limited; a group of people is another matter entirely.
entry.summary: What five years on a UK academic post taught me about European academia — the job system, its pros and cons, and what we built at Glasgow.

entry.title: MSc in Urban Analytics study visit to Aalto University in Finland

link.title: MSc in Urban Analytics study visit to Aalto University in Finland

entry.content:
Originally published on the Urban Big Data Centre blog in 2024 (by the MSc Urban Analytics visiting group and Qunshan Zhao).

Four UBDC students on our MSc in Urban Analytics programme have recently returned from a rewarding study visit to Aalto University near Helsinki, Finland.

Geoinformatics is a multidisciplinary field at Aalto University and their research and applications cover a wide range of themes from human-centred to natural environments. In Aalto, the research areas include geodesy, geoinformation technology, digital photogrammetry and laser scanning, and remote sensing.

Dr Henrikki Tenkanen from the Department of Built Environment welcomed the visiting group and led their itinerary. Dr Tenkanen heads the GIST Lab which aims to advance research, teaching and open-source scientific software development around a variety of topics such as: GIScience; spatial accessibility and mobility analysis; sustainable use of water resources; cybersecurity around open spatial data; and software for advancing open science.

The study visit provided an opportunity for the next generation of urban analytics students from Glasgow to develop their networks and exchange ideas.
entry.summary: Reposted from the Urban Big Data Centre blog.

entry.title: Improving the P-Median model with capacity and near-far cost allocation in spopt

link.title: Improving the P-Median model with capacity and near-far cost allocation in spopt

entry.content:
Originally published on the Urban Big Data Centre blog on 11 September 2023 (by Rongbo Xu and the spopt mentor team (including Qunshan Zhao)). The original page is no longer online — an archived copy is available on the Wayback Machine, including the figures omitted here.

Spopt is an open-source Python library for solving optimization problems with spatial data (Feng et al., 2022). Originating from the region module in PySAL (Python Spatial Analysis Library), it is actively evolving to incorporate recently proposed models and techniques aimed at solving problems related to regionalization, facility location, and transportation.

Following the development of the facility location modelling module for spopt in 2021 by Germano, Rongbo Xu participated in the Google Summer of Code programme in 2023, and contributed valuable enhancements to the specific location-allocation model in spopt. These enhancements can be divided into two main components: the implementation of capacity constraints in the current p-median models and the creation of a novel model incorporating near-far cost allocation principles. Notably, the project team comprises Rongbo and five mentors.

In this blog, the project team explains the two improvements on the p-median model developed by Rongbo and shares the future plans for this package.

Part 1: Enhancement - The P-median Model with Capacity Constraints

Background to the project

After years of continuous development, the ‘locate’ component of the spopt package has successfully delivered numerous essential and classical coverage and location-allocation models. These models include the Location Set Covering Problem (LSCP), Maximal Coverage Location Problem (MCLP), p-median Problem, p-dispersion Problem, and p-center Problem. We also have developed more advanced models such as the backup coverage location Problem with LSCP and included other relevant parameters such as facility capacities and predefined facilities for the coverage models. Nevertheless, the real world presents diverse scenarios and challenges that demand specific requirements and constraints.

The genesis of this project can be traced back to the Institute of Education (IOE) at University College London (UCL), where there was a pressing need to enhance their student placement process. In this particular instance, the existing ‘p-median’ module within spopt proved inadequate. The challenge at hand necessitated the creation of a capacitated variant of the traditional P-median Problem. This variation explicitly takes into account not only the locations and weights of clients but also the capacity constraints of the facilities poised to serve these clients.

In accordance with the specifications provided by UCL IOE, Rongbu initially devised the capacitated p-median model. UCL IOE faces the task of placing approximately 800 students twice a year, and they have a pool of roughly 500 schools available for placements. The primary goal is to minimize the cumulative travel time for all students by using public transport. This objective is contingent upon ensuring that each student is assigned to a school offering instruction in their respective subject, all while accounting for the capacity and priority level of each school.

We have received support for this project from AGILE initiative, and you can find the corresponding initiative (https://web.archive.org/web/2022/https://agile-online.org/agile-actions/initiatives/development-of-open-source-python-modelling-package) and paper (Bearman et al., 2023) here.

Results and Conclusions

Once we had implemented the capacitated p-median model, we conducted a series of tests utilizing sample data before applying it to the actual dataset provided by UCL IOE. The outcomes generated by the initial version of the capacitated p-median location-allocation model demonstrated a significant enhancement in the total public transport travel time for students. Specifically, there was a reduction of 1194 minutes in overall public transport travel time for an initial sample of 106 students, translating to an average reduction of 11.3 minutes per student.

As depicted in Figure 1, the analysis reveals that the majority of students experienced a reduction in their travel time when compared to the travel times determined manually by the UCL IOE staff. Some students have a slight increase of their travel time (mostly less than 20 mins), and all the results have been shared with the UCL IOE for further examination. The development of the capacitated p-median problem has been submitted and merged into the spopt package.

Figure 1. The histogram of the difference between optimised travel time and previous travel time

Part 2: Development - The P-median Model with Near-far Cost Allocation

Background to the project

The introduction of the P-median Model with Near-far Cost Allocation represents a notable enhancement in model efficiency, particularly addressing scenarios involving large volumes of data, which have become increasingly prevalent in today’s context. Traditional location allocation models typically involve processing extensive information between all the clients and facilities, calculating distances, and then evaluating the optimal solution from a vast pool of potential solutions, sometimes numbering in the thousands or even millions. This often results in excessively lengthy computation times.

The k nearest p-median problem extends the concept of facility location allocation by considering both nearest and non-nearest facilities. In the article by Church (2018), it proposed this new p-median model which can distinguish between near and far facilities, using both explicit and implicit variables for capacity allocations. By integrating this model, the spopt package becomes equipped to deliver heightened precision and efficiency in solving spatial optimization problems, especially those involving extensive datasets and substantial demand volumes.

The notebook introduces the formulation of this new model, the technical details of its implements in spopt package, and a guide on how to use it.

Model and solution approach

This model introduces a clever approach by differentiating between nearby and distant facilities. For instance, at the beginning, only a given number of the closest facilities (set as 5) to each client are considered, which are termed as “near” facilities. If all client demands and constraints can be satisfied, the problem will have an optimal solution right away. However, if the initial facilities can’t meet all the demands, we can supplement them with additional nearby facilities.

Mathematically, we create a variable called the placeholder facility decision variable, which is 𝑔𝑖 in the formula, presenting the distant facilities (Church, 2018).

In terms of the solution approach:

  1. For each client, identify their five closest facilities.

  2. Run the p-median model only for the selected facilities from Step 1 (referred to as “explicitly assigned” in the paper).

  3. Check the model outcome. If all 𝑔𝑖 values for clients are equal to 0 and an optimal solution exists, we accept this solution.

  4. If some 𝑔𝑖 values are more than 0, we need to increase the value 𝑘 for specific clients. Then, rerun the model. Ensure that all 𝑔𝑖 values become 0, indicating an optimal solution.

The default starting value for 𝑘 is 5, or the total number of facilities if it is fewer than 5. Nevertheless, users have the flexibility to customize this value according to their particular requirements.

This model allows us to avoid incorporating the complete set of distances and facilities into the model. Instead, we can focus on integrating smaller segments of these elements and utilize iteration to identify the optimal solution, thereby enhancing computational speed and efficiency.

Model evaluation and validation

To validate the k nearest p-median model, we constructed a synthetic example specifically for testing purposes. Figure 2 provides a visual representation of the spatial distribution of demand and facility points within this synthetic example. We initialized the attributes as follows:

  1. Each demand point possesses a demand volume of 1.

  2. The capacity of each facility is set to 1.

  3. The initial value of ‘k’ is established at 1.

In this scenario, it becomes evident that the model with ‘k’ equal to 1 will not yield an optimal solution. This is because the two closely located demand points share the same facility as their nearest one, yet each facility can serve only one client. Consequently, the solution process necessitates proceeding to Step 4, and it becomes necessary to increase the ‘k’ value for certain demand points to 2.

Figure 2. The scatter plot of the demand points and facilities points in the synthetic example.

The outcomes align with our expectations, and you can further observe them in the following notebook (https://github.com/rongboxu/P-Median-Model-with-Near-Far-Cost-Allocation/blob/main/notebooks/k_nearest_p_median_model.ipynb). We successfully obtained the optimal solution, including the appropriate ‘k’ value for each demand point. In Figure 3, you can observe the outcome of the allocation process. The green arrows clearly indicate which facility point is assigned to serve each demand point. In the case of the two closely located demand points, one of them is served by the nearest facility (represented by the central red point), while the other is served by its second nearest facility (indicated by the red point with ‘y’ coordinates of 2).

Figure 3. The allocation results of the synthetic example.

Part 3: Future Plans

Future development on spopt will provide additional models and novel variations of existing models in both region and locate. The capacity constraint and the solution idea of near-far cost allocation may also be implemented on other existing models.

Final thoughts on the project from Rongbo:

“Participating in GSoC has been incredibly rewarding, especially in achieving my goal of introducing new functionality. I find the collaborative nature of open-source coding fascinating and enjoy working with fellow team members to make meaningful contributions! Reflecting on my journey, I remember feeling uncertain at the beginning and gradually overcoming challenges. GSoC has been instrumental in boosting my programming skills, fostering a deep understanding of software development practices, and preparing me for future coding challenges. I’m also truly thankful to my mentors for guiding me in the right direction. As these months have passed, I’ve successfully implemented the changes I aimed for. Completing this project leaves me feeling happy, satisfied, and confident in both the project’s outcome and my personal growth.”

Project Team

GSOC 2023

● Rongbo Xu, Individual, UK

● Dr Levi John Wolf, School of Geographical Sciences, University of Bristol, UK

● Dr James Gaboardi, Geospatial Science and Human Security Division, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, USA

● Prof Sergio Rey, Center for Open Geographical Science, San Diego State University, USA

● Dr Qunshan Zhao, UBDC and Urban Studies, University of Glasgow, UK

● Germano Barcelos, NESPeD-LAB, Universidade Federal de Viçosa, Brazil

 

AGILE initiative

● Rongbo Xu, University of Glasgow, UK

● Dr Qunshan Zhao, UBDC and Urban Studies, University of Glasgow, UK

● Dr Huanfa Chen, Centre for Advanced Spatial Analysis, University College London, UK

● Dr Nick Bearman, Geospatial Training Solutions and University College London, UK

● Dr Levi John Wolf, School of Geographical Sciences, University of Bristol, UK

● Dr James Gaboardi, Geospatial Science and Human Security Division, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, USA

 

Reference

Bearman, N., Xu, R., Roddy, P. J., Gaboardi, J. D., Zhao, Q., Chen, H., & Wolf, L. (2023). Developing capacitated p-median location-allocation model in the spopt library to allow UCL student teacher placements using public transport. AGILE: GIScience Series, 4, 1–7. 

Church, R. L. (2018). Tobler’s Law and Spatial Optimization: Why Bakersfield? International Regional Science Review, 41(3), 287–310. https://doi.org/10.1177/0160017616650612

Feng, X., Barcelos, G., Gaboardi, J. D., Knaap, E., Wei, R., Wolf, L. J., Zhao, Q., & Rey, S. J. (2022). spopt: A python package for solving spatial optimization problems in PySAL. Journal of Open Source Software, 7(74), 3330.
entry.summary: Reposted from the Urban Big Data Centre blog.

entry.title: 2022年年终总结：新常态
entry.content:
本文首发于知乎。English version: read here.

今天是今年最后一个工作日,明天就要启程去摩洛哥度假啦!很难想象这只是来英国之后第一次出国。除去懒得办签证的原因,剩下的就全是被疫情困在岛上了。

刚才翻看了一下去年的总结,发现愿望基本上都实现了,除了还没去成欧陆。今年开了5个英国境内的会,GISRUK2022(利物浦),IMGS2022(爱丁堡),ABCP2022 (伯明翰),RGS2022 (纽卡),还有CATE2022(爱丁堡)。其中IMGS和CATE都是正好在爱丁堡办,国家小有个好处就是凑热闹容易。加上学生和博后,今年总共做了六次会议报告,自己还做了五次的受邀学术讲座。明年我还参与了三项大型学术会议的组织承办,包括在格拉斯哥的GISRUK2023,在伦敦的CPGIS2023,和在利兹的GIScience2023。如果大家有时间有经费有签证欢迎来英国转转。

今年文章发的比去年稍微多一些,4篇期刊发表,4篇在审,还有3篇会议论文。其实项目里面还是堆积了一些文章没时间修改和投稿,就等明年再说吧。项目经费方面相对来说比较成功,一共中了2个ESRC资助的博士生项目 (1PI+1Co-I),1个300k的SDAI(中心一起合作的博后是PI),1.6m盖茨基金会的非洲项目(Co-I),格拉斯哥市政府的3D city modeling的项目(PI),还有两个资助城市分析硕士的教学方向的小项目(PI)。临近年底又拿到了AGILE funding initiatives的项目 (PI),可以接着今年GSoC的项目继续做spopt的开源包开发。加上之前UBDC的项目林林总总的倒是不少,后面就看怎么转化成学术成果和社会影响力了。

今年一直在打广告招人,博后教职都招了三四轮了,明年会有不少新面孔加入我们。目前还有2个博后和3个教职开放,明年年初还有一个和Ideamaps主题相关的非洲城市遥感全奖博士生奖学金,欢迎大家关注:赵群山:格拉斯哥大学城市研究系与城市大数据中心招聘 (12 月 20 日更新)。

教学方面,22年春天因为还有一些国际学生没有来英国,教学的时候除了用提前录好的视频之外,还得同步现场和zoom直播。今年秋天教课就基本上全部恢复到疫情前了,几乎用了快三年时间才回到常态。

除了正常在格大的工作之外,今年还多了一些校外的兼职工作,包括参与了ESRC grant assessment panel,给华威大学做它们Urban Analytics MSc 最后一年的external examiner,还有参加ESRC future data service programme advisory board。和funder打交道越多就感觉其实它们内部的玩法也很新鲜,存在很多资源竞争和妥协谈判。ESRC grant assessment panel 是特别有用的一个经历,可以看到一个研究proposal是怎么样通过层层的筛选,最后走到拿到funding的时刻。中间不可控的因素很多,给大家的建议就是要好好打磨研究思路和本子,对于所有的环节(尤其是审稿意见回复)都要认真对待,最后就是wish for luck了(等我什么时候有空可以写篇文章专门讲讲这个英国grant assessment的过程,还是很有意思的)。

最后可能要说一下是今年升了副教授。要感谢格大领导同事们的支持和鼓励,还有一起合作的博士和博士后的帮助,继续往下一个目标迈进。

别的方面,希望中国大陆的这波疫情能够平稳度过,明年逐渐放开隔离政策和航班数量恢复正常以后打算回国一次(上次离开中国是2016年1月了)。俄乌战争没有停下来的意思,欧洲能源价格高企,美联储英国欧洲央行加息不断,全球经济衰退就在眼前。祝大家身体健康,工作稳定,珍惜和家人朋友在一起的时光,期待明年认识更多的新朋友,再和老朋友重逢!
entry.summary: 首发于知乎的中文原文;English version available.

entry.title: 2022 Year in Review: The New Normal

link.title: 2022 Year in Review: The New Normal

entry.content:
Translated from my original Chinese post on Zhihu (published December 2022). 中文版

Today is the last working day of the year — tomorrow I set off for a holiday in Morocco! Hard to believe this is my first trip abroad since coming to the UK. Aside from being too lazy to sort out visas, the rest of the blame lies entirely with being stranded on this island by the pandemic.

I just re-read last year’s summary and found that the wishes basically all came true — except that I still have not made it to continental Europe. This year I attended five conferences within the UK: GISRUK 2022 (Liverpool), IMGS 2022 (Edinburgh), ABCP 2022 (Birmingham), RGS 2022 (Newcastle), and CATE 2022 (Edinburgh). IMGS and CATE both happened to be in Edinburgh — one upside of a small country is that joining the fun is easy. Counting students and postdocs, we gave six conference presentations in total, and I gave five invited academic talks myself. Next year I am involved in organising three major academic conferences: GISRUK 2023 in Glasgow, CPGIS 2023 in London, and GIScience 2023 in Leeds. If you have the time, the funding, and the visa, do come and have a look around the UK.

Publications came out slightly ahead of last year: 4 journal papers published, 4 under review, and 3 conference papers. There is still a backlog of papers sitting inside projects waiting to be revised and submitted — that will have to wait for next year. On the funding side it was a rather successful year: two ESRC-funded PhD studentship projects (one as PI, one as Co-I), a £300k SDAI grant (with a postdoc collaborator at the centre as PI), a £1.6m Gates Foundation project in Africa (Co-I), a 3D city modelling project for Glasgow City Council (PI), and two small teaching-focused grants supporting the Urban Analytics MSc (PI). Right at the end of the year an AGILE funding initiative grant (PI) landed as well, which lets us continue the spopt open-source development following this year’s GSoC project. Together with the existing UBDC projects, that adds up to quite a lot — the question now is how to turn it all into academic output and societal impact.

We advertised jobs all year long — three or four rounds of postdoc and faculty recruitment — so there will be plenty of new faces joining us next year. There are still 2 postdoc and 3 faculty positions open, plus a fully funded PhD studentship on African urban remote sensing (related to the IDEAMAPS theme) opening early next year.

On teaching: in spring 2022, with some international students still unable to come to the UK, I taught with pre-recorded videos plus a simultaneous in-person and Zoom livestream. By this autumn teaching had basically returned to pre-pandemic normality — it took almost three years to get back to normal.

Beyond my regular work at Glasgow, I took on some external roles this year: joining an ESRC grant assessment panel, serving as the final-year external examiner for Warwick’s Urban Analytics MSc, and sitting on the ESRC Future Data Services programme advisory board. The more I deal with funders, the more novel their internal workings seem — full of resource competition and negotiated compromise. The ESRC grant assessment panel was a particularly useful experience: you get to see how a research proposal passes through layer after layer of screening to the moment it finally wins funding. There are many uncontrollable factors along the way. My advice: polish your research ideas and your proposal properly, take every stage seriously (especially responses to reviewer comments), and after that — wish for luck. (One day when I have time I should write a piece specifically about the UK grant assessment process; it really is interesting.)

Finally, I should mention that I was promoted to Senior Lecturer (Associate Professor) this year. My thanks to the leadership and colleagues at Glasgow for their support and encouragement, and to the PhD students and postdocs I work with — onwards to the next goal.

On other fronts: I hope mainland China comes through this wave of the pandemic smoothly; once quarantine policies relax and flights recover next year, I plan to go back to China for a visit (I last left China in January 2016). The war in Ukraine shows no sign of stopping, European energy prices are sky-high, and with the Fed, the Bank of England, and the ECB raising rates relentlessly, a global recession is looming. I wish everyone good health and stable work — treasure the time with family and friends. Here’s to meeting more new friends next year, and to reunions with old ones!
entry.summary: Conferences returned, grants landed, teaching went back to normal, and a promotion to Senior Lecturer — a year-end summary.

entry.title: Reviewing the reproducibility of research at AGILE PhD School

link.title: Reviewing the reproducibility of research at AGILE PhD School

entry.content:
Originally published on the Urban Big Data Centre blog on 1 September 2022 (by Yue Li, Rafael Verduzco Torres, Qunshan Zhao and Jing Yao). The original page is no longer online — an archived copy is available on the Wayback Machine, including the figures omitted here.

Three of our PhD students recently participated in the AGILE PhD School - a forum for the next generation of scientists and research leaders to develop their networks and exchange ideas.

The Urban Big Data Centre joined the Association of Geographic Information Laboratories in Europe (AGILE) in November 2019 to represent the University of Glasgow in the European GIScience academic community, along with the University of St Andrews, University of Leeds, University of Nottingham, and the University of Leicester in the United Kingdom. A full list of AGILE members appears on their website.

AGILE was established in 1998 with a mission to promote the geographic information teaching and research community and ensure that the community’s views are fully represented at the European level. They organise initiatives on specific topics to influence the future European geographic information research agenda.

The 6th AGILE PhD School took place in June in Castellón, Spain, with the theme make your work more open and reproducible. There were training sessions on academic writing and presenting/pitching skills and introductions to open science and reproducibility/replicability in GIScience. The students also participated in discussions about what open science means for their work, which aimed to address their concerns and barriers to openness.

In this blog, Yue Li and Rafael Verduzco share their experiences, and Dr Qunshan Zhao and Dr Jing Yao summarise the importance of this type of activity for our PhD students.

Yue’s experience

This was the first time I had participated in this type of academic activity, which enabled me to connect with other PhD students from Europe. We not only presented our research to date, exchanged ideas, and acquired and improved skills related to Geoscience, but also made friendships and developed our network with each other.

Specifically, the three-minute thesis presentation exposed us to the diversity of fields and common features of our domain and provided the opportunity to exchange ideas about the PhD process. Also, organising our research into a short presentation allowed us to better understand the research objectives. Meanwhile, as a person with Glossophobia, I always fear public speaking, and the three-minute presentation offered me a good opportunity to overcome it.

In addition, we had a group debate on open science and reproducibility’s impact on early-stage researchers, which encouraged me to improve my research reproducibility in the future.

Overall, I was fortunate to participate in the PhD school and to meet a very kind and passionate group of people from different countries. I would like to thank UBDC and AGILE, and I hope to participate in more interesting academic events like this in the future.

Rafa’s experience

I took away several lessons directly applicable to my PhD experience and overall work.

After a clear exposition of the situation in the field by the hosts, we evaluated published scientific work from the lens of reproducibility. This exercise is relevant to thinking about our research too. In fact, we developed a plan aimed at improving the level of reproducibility of our ongoing PhD work. This was reviewed by the experienced organisers, and I am currently implementing this plan.

Furthermore, the review of the principles for Open Science and reproducibility was useful for the improvement of a UBDC project about Accessibility Indicators for Great Britain, which was recently published. All the inputs, processes, and outputs are published as open and reproducible as possible, including open code, an open access technical report, and open access to the main indicators via the UBDC data repository.

Lastly, the networking, discussion, and friendships established with my European peers are invaluable for widening my perspective of academic work.

This is certainly a must for any doctoral student researching topics related to GIS.

Dr Qunshan Zhao’s feedback

I think the AGILE PhD School is a great opportunity for all the PhD students in Europe to connect and collaborate in a friendly and open-minded environment. After almost 2.5 years of the pandemic, PhD students need to resume these offline activities to build up their academic networks and receive training from these events. I also believe the topics this year were timely and useful. Soft skills, such as academic writing and presentation, are vital for every aspect of academic life, while open science (reproducibility and replicability) practices are widely encouraged by all the research funders and academic institutions. The AGILE PhD school provides initial training for our early career researchers that will benefit them significantly in their future careers in academia.

This is a unique benefit for the AGILE membership institution, and I would strongly encourage all the PhD students in the AGILE community to consider applying to this PhD school (all fees waived with travel support!). I also hope that more academic institutions in the UK will join the AGILE community in the future.

Dr Jing Yao’s feedback

The AGILE PhD School provides a perfect platform for PhD students within the AGILE community. It offers them a unique opportunity for exchanging research ideas and discussing geospatial methods with their peers from universities across Europe, as well as developing personal networks. There is no doubt that this will benefit their PhD studies and future career. Such experience often cannot be obtained from students’ own universities/institutions. Also, the AGILE PhD School has a particular theme every year which relates to an emerging topic of wide concern in geoinformatics. The theme of this year was reproducibility and replicability, which was welcomed by many scholars. However, there are still many barriers to this. Group debate on this theme can help students better understand the advantages of open science and reproducibility and the related concerns.

I am glad to see three of our PhD students attend the AGILE PhD School this year, and Yue and Rafa agreed that the experience is invaluable to their PhD studies and future career development. I would encourage PhD students from AGILE membership institutions to attend the PhD School in the future and hope they all can benefit from it.

 

If you are looking for research and studying opportunities with us, please see our PhD Research and MSc Programmes pages.
entry.summary: Three of our PhD students recently participated in the AGILE PhD School - a forum for the next generation of scientists and research leaders to develop their networks and exchange ideas.

entry.title: 关于英国 PSW/HPI 签证的一些讨论

link.title: 关于英国 PSW/HPI 签证的一些讨论

entry.content:
本文首发于知乎。English version: read here.

前段时间英国正式开放了新的 high potential individual (HPI) 的签证申请,引来了很多英国的公众号还有媒体的关注。符合条件的大学的 list 也得到了大家的频繁讨论(吐槽)。我本来觉得这个政策可能并不会吸引来太多的人,但是最近逛一亩三分地的时候居然看到了好几个关于这个签证的讨论,今天就来谈谈我的一些简单想法。

这个 HPI 签证其实是 PSW 签证的国际版本。先说说 PSW 签证。PSW 签证大概十年前被梅姨取消,大致是类似于一个英国版的不限制专业的OPT,英国大学的本科硕士博士生在毕业之后可以有 2 年(博士 3 年)的合法工作的期限,工作内容不限,极端一点可以说去餐馆打工 2 年或者自己做生意 2 年也可以。申请这个签证不需要有工作,毕业就能申请,不能用来积累永居的时间(十年永居应该可以),也不可以延长。如果想要在 2-3 年之后继续留在英国工作,需要换成常规的工签比如 tier2 或者 global talent visa (原来的 tier1)。总之就是英国脱欧以后人才精英化的一个新举措。

HPI 是进一步的把 PSW 签证拓展到全世界的顶尖大学,具体是过去五年(实际上有 6 年的排名)从英国政府指定的大学 list 里面毕业的学生可以申请,这里面是没有英国的学校的(因为英国学校已经有psw了,不管你学校排名前十还是几百都能申请)。这过去 5-6 年的 list 里面的学校会有一些变化,当然大家最认可的世界 top 30 肯定都有,就是估计排名 40-50 的会有点波动。这个排名因为是从三大世界大学排名里面(QS,THE,ARWU?(不太确定))必须有 2 个排在世界综合排名前五十,肯定是比较倾向英语国家的学校的(比如美国,澳洲,加拿大等等)。像欧洲的很多好学校都不在这个 list 里面(大概只有瑞士的 top2 和德国法国瑞典的几所可以上榜,没有荷兰我就觉得很瞎),对于一些专业排名高但是综排低的学校也不是很友好。亚洲地区的包括中国大陆 top2,新加坡 top2,港大(港科有时候有),东京大学和京都大学。这个 HPI 签证和 PSW 签证一样,本身是不能申请永居也不能延长的,有点类似荷兰的 search year visa。

签证主页在这:

High Potential Individual (HPI) visa

排名在这:

High Potential Individual (HPI) visa eligibility

我个人觉得出 PSW 和 HPI 签证当然是对想要留英的学生的利好,毕竟可以有更多的时间找工作,也可以从别的国家直接登陆英国然后开始工作。很多英国的政府或者一些小公司的工作不太 sponsor tier 2,有这个签证可以作为一个过渡在英国多积累工作经验和体验 2-3 年生活,也可以再找可以 sponsor 转永居的签证。我自己的硕士项目里面今年就有一个学生还没毕业就拿到了苏格兰政府这边的一个偏数据的岗位,然后申请 PSW 签证来工作。他和我说如果没有这个 PSW 签证,那这个岗位就没法给他了。

至于英国的工作肯定盘子是小多了,金融是主体,IT 公司除了美国的大厂分部,小厂有一些,数据科学方面的工作不少。制造业不多比如有 RR, Dyson, ARM, 还有一些车厂(阿斯顿马丁和捷豹),还有医药行业比如葛兰素史克和阿斯利康,大学里工作也不少,大家也开始慢慢摸索利用这个签证从国外来招一些短期的研究和教学职位。

这个签证对有博士学位的人吸引力不是非常大,因为如果做博后基本上走 globa talent visa 或者 tier2 就行了,不用在这自己折腾。我觉得 PSW 的意义还是比这个 HPI 会大得多,因为如果已经在英国读了几年书,再留 2 年各方面也都比较熟悉方便。但是 HPI 是从另外一个国家过来,而且基本上都是国际上顶尖的学校。从这些学校出来的学生在本国肯定也能找到不错的工作。如果之前没有在英国读过书或者有人脉,单纯的来2年工作和生活其实是个不小的挑战。而且很多人来英国是已经拿到工作并且可以申请工签才会考虑。至于 HPI 的具体成效可以等等看明年英国官方的数据,看看有多少人申请了这个新的项目。

英国自从脱欧以后,从欧洲来的学生和移民骤降。吸引高素质人才肯定是好举措,但是怎么弥补原本东欧南欧过来的基本劳动力会是一个长期问题。
entry.summary: 首发于知乎的中文原文;English version available.

entry.title: Some Thoughts on the UK PSW and HPI Visas

link.title: Some Thoughts on the UK PSW and HPI Visas

entry.content:
Translated from my original Chinese post on Zhihu (published June 2022). 中文版

The UK recently opened applications for the new High Potential Individual (HPI) visa, which drew plenty of attention from UK-focused social media accounts and the press. The list of qualifying universities has been much discussed (and mocked). I initially thought the policy would not attract many people, but recently I saw several threads about this visa while browsing 1point3acres, so here are some quick thoughts.

The HPI visa is essentially an international version of the PSW visa. First, the PSW. The PSW visa was scrapped by Theresa May about a decade ago; it was roughly a British version of an unrestricted OPT: graduates of UK bachelor’s, master’s, or doctoral programmes could work legally for 2 years (3 years for PhDs) in any kind of job — at the extreme, you could spend two years waiting tables or running your own business. You do not need a job to apply — graduating is enough. It cannot be counted towards (five-year) settlement (the ten-year route should still count it), and it cannot be extended. If you want to stay and work beyond the 2–3 years, you need to switch to a regular work visa such as Tier 2 or the Global Talent Visa (the old Tier 1). In short, it is one of the UK’s post-Brexit measures to skim off talent.

The HPI extends the PSW idea to top universities worldwide: graduates from the past five years (in practice six years of rankings) of universities on a UK government list can apply. UK universities are not on the list (their graduates already have the PSW, whether the school ranks in the top ten or the hundreds). The list changes a bit across those five–six years; the universally recognised world top 30 are all on it, while schools ranked around 40–50 fluctuate. Because eligibility requires being in the top 50 of at least two of the three major world rankings (QS, THE, ARWU — I am not entirely sure), the list clearly favours universities in English-speaking countries (the US, Australia, Canada, and so on). Many excellent European universities are missing (roughly only Switzerland’s top two and a few in Germany, France, and Sweden make it — the absence of the Netherlands strikes me as absurd), and it is unfriendly to schools with top subject rankings but lower overall rankings. From Asia, the list includes mainland China’s top two, Singapore’s top two, HKU (HKUST some years), and the Universities of Tokyo and Kyoto. Like the PSW, the HPI itself cannot lead to settlement and cannot be extended — somewhat like the Dutch “search year” visa.

The visa homepage is here: High Potential Individual (HPI) visa. The rankings/eligibility are here: HPI visa eligibility.

Personally, I think the PSW and HPI are clearly good news for students who want to stay in the UK: more time to find a job, and the option to land in the UK directly from another country and start working. Many UK government jobs and smaller companies are reluctant to sponsor Tier 2; these visas provide a transition — two or three years to build UK work experience and try out life here, while looking for a sponsored route to settlement. In my own master’s programme this year, one student got a data-focused role with the Scottish Government before even graduating and used the PSW to take it up. He told me that without the PSW, the role simply could not have been offered to him.

The UK job market is of course much smaller. Finance is the mainstay; in IT, beyond branches of the US giants there are some smaller firms, and data science jobs are plentiful. Manufacturing is limited — Rolls-Royce, Dyson, ARM, some carmakers (Aston Martin, Jaguar) — plus pharma (GSK, AstraZeneca). Universities also offer plenty of jobs, and people are beginning to use this visa to recruit short-term research and teaching staff from abroad.

For PhD holders the visa is not hugely attractive: if you are doing a postdoc, the Global Talent Visa or Tier 2 route works fine without the extra hassle. I think the PSW matters far more than the HPI: if you have already studied in the UK for a few years, staying another two is familiar and convenient. The HPI, by contrast, means arriving from another country — and mostly from the world’s top universities, whose graduates can certainly find good jobs at home. Without prior UK study or networks, coming over for a two-year stint of work and life is no small challenge; and many people only consider the UK once they already have a job offer and work-visa sponsorship. As for how effective the HPI proves, we can wait for next year’s official statistics to see how many people actually applied.

Since Brexit, students and migrants from Europe have plummeted. Attracting high-skilled talent is certainly a good move — but how to replace the basic labour force that used to come from Eastern and Southern Europe will be a long-term problem.
entry.summary: What the Graduate (PSW) route and the new High Potential Individual visa actually offer — and who they are really for.

entry.title: Urban Analytics students dive into urban data

link.title: Urban Analytics students dive into urban data

entry.content:
Originally published on the Urban Big Data Centre blog on 22 April 2022 (by Jorge Michel, Samuel Hönle and Qunshan Zhao). The original page is no longer online — an archived copy is available on the Wayback Machine, including the figures omitted here.

Four of our MSc in Urban Analytics students took part in the Centre for Urban Science and Progress London (CUSP London) Data Dive on 4-7 April with the theme of Working Towards Healthier Cities. As you can imagine, it was right up their street.

Fifty students from five universities were split into groups and tasked with tackling one of three challenges: Making sense of simulations of an infectious disease outbreak in Manchester; finding patterns in Stop and Search police data; combining COVID-19 data with other data sets to gain insights and to find correlations. They only had a few days to explore the data available and produce analyses to develop understanding and help deliver practical solutions to the challenges facing city-dwellers everyday lives.

UBDC MSc students Jorge Michel Gutierrez and Samuel Hönle explain the challenges they took part in and share their experiences below.

Jorge’s experience

 

The stop and search challenge

I was fortunate to collaborate with a very smart and passionate group of people from UCL and King’s College London, who had different bachelor’s backgrounds, including music, data science, computer science and communication. However, we all shared similar master’s programmes, such as urban informatics, urban analytics, or spatial analysis. We chose to work on the crime challenge, where the main objective was to analyse stop and search data and understand its efficiency in crime reduction and prevention. Stop and search is a procedure conducted by the police if they have ‘reasonable grounds’ to suspect a person is carrying illegal drugs, a weapon, stolen property, or something which could be used to commit a crime. The data used was from UK data police, specifically crime and stop and search data, the English Index of Multiple Deprivation (IMD) and ONS census data.

 

Our approach

We approached the problem from two perspectives. The first looked at possible demographics and socioeconomic biases that could be impacting the accuracy of stop and search and, therefore, the actual crime reduction. The second was to understand if the accuracy of stop and search had an impact on reducing crime. Lower Layer Super Output Areas (LSOAs) were taken as areas of analysis for both. For the first approach, a racial discrepancy metric was developed, which was the difference between the proportion of the non-white population in an area and the proportion of non-white people stopped and searched by the police in the same area. This was mapped to find if there was any pattern and if there was a relation to the crime reduction.

As shown in the maps above, we found that the non-white population is overrepresented in stop and searches in many LSOAs, with only very few areas where the opposite happens. We found a discrepancy ring around central London. However, there was no significant negative impact of the discrepancy on crime prevention, as many of the LSOAs presenting this overrepresentation still had a reduction in average crime from 2019 to 2021.

On the other hand, when looking at accuracy - the rate in which the outcome of a stop and search procedure was detention because those searched were carrying something illegal - we found that they have a measurable impact on crime reduction. However, the volume of stop and searches by itself had four times more impact on crime reduction than accuracy. This was concluded after a regression model where the IMD and the non-white population was included to control for sociodemographic factors that could have influenced the crime trends. When linking both findings - the racial discrepancy and accuracy - there was a very low correlation between them, as seen in the figure above.

 

Conclusions

Based on the findings, we concluded that increasing the number of stop and searches can benefit crime prevention to a limited extent but after a certain point it becomes inefficient, and the reinforcement of racial discrepancies could override the benefits. To improve accuracy in procedures, police should look at factors such as deprivation of an area to determine the number of stop and searches that should be conducted.

Samuel’s experience

 

The COVID-19 challenge

The challenge brief was formulated very broadly, leaving the team with a lot of freedom in defining what we wanted to study. We decided to focus on the impact of policy decisions on COVID-19 infection numbers. Specifically, we wanted to know whether lifting the containment policy during the decline in infections in early 2022 measurably led to an increase in cases in London.

 

Our approach

We combined COVID-19 infection data for London with a policy index for different pandemic response measures in England compiled by the University of Oxford. We then used interrupted time-series regression analysis to see which policy changes in early 2022 had a measurable effect on new infections. With that information, we went back to the last time measures were eased in mid-2021 and compared the results. By limiting the study both spatially and temporally, we tried to fix as many other effects as possible but emphasised that wider contextual knowledge of the pandemic was necessary to further interpret our results. We then compiled our findings to advise a policy maker on which policies to lift first after a hypothetical future COVID-19 wave.

 

Conclusions

We found that:

  • Lifting mask requirements completely, together with other measures, with a more transmissible variant in winter, significantly increased spread.

  • Lifting the same restrictions in summer with a less transmissible variant and some mask requirements remaining, did not significantly increase the spread of the virus.

  • School openings and allowing international travel had no direct measurable impact (early 2022).

One interesting feature of the process was the format of the presentations. The teams were challenged to limit their presentations to four slides:

  • Slide one: a description of the problem, the data, and the limitations of the analysis

  • Slide two: the findings, containing clear key insights or recommendations for policy makers

  • Slide three: a visual representation of the results

  • Slide four: any additional necessary insights into the data or the methods

Nick Holliman, Professor of Computer Science and Director of CUSP, explained that this was the most effective way to communicate research to decision-makers when tested with the British Army.

I am proud to say that my team received the Best Technical Contribution Award for our analysis and presentation (PDF 0.98MB). You can view the code behind our analysis on GitHub.

Image: Winners of the Best Technical Contribution Award. From left to right: Elika Sinha, Yuning Jiang, Tongqing Zhu (also an MSc in Urban Analytics student), and Samuel Hönle.

The CUSP London Data Dive 2022 gave students the chance to apply the skills we gained in our studies to real-world data while focussing on producing useful results and presenting them in an innovative format. Participating in the event gave me confidence in the skills and education we receive at the University of Glasgow. And finally, participating in person meant that we could connect with fellow students in our field from other universities and our potential future colleagues, and enjoy four days in London together with them.

A word from the MSc in Urban Analytics programme convener - Dr Qunshan Zhao

This year is the first time our MSc in Urban Analytics students have taken part in the CUSP Data Dive in London. The event provides a perfect opportunity for students to apply what they have learnt from our programme while under pressure in a fast-paced environment. As a result, our students improve their understanding of how data science is applied in real-world situations and develop practical skills in formulating questions, analysing data, and presenting results. We plan to continue participating in these events, which will provide a unique learning and networking experience for our future students.

If you like what you’ve read and you’re interested in studying with us, please visit our MSc in Urban Analytics programme page where you can find out more and apply.
entry.summary: Four of our MSc in Urban Analytics students took part in the Centre for Urban Science and Progress London (CUSP London) Data Dive on 4-7 April with the theme of Working Towards Healthier Cities. As you can imagine, it...
